passages.title: UNDOING OUR EMOTIONS
passages.source_html:
UNDOING OUR EMOTIONS


A


Three generations ago, 180 young women wrote essays describing why they wanted to join a convent (a religious community of nuns). Years later, a team of psychological researchers came across these autobiographies in the convent’s archives. The researchers were seeking material to confirm earlier studies hinting at a link between having a good vocabulary in youth and a low risk of Alzheimer’s disease in old age. What they found was even more amazing.The researchers found that, although the young women were in their early twenties when they wrote their essays, the emotions expressed in these writings were predictive of how long they would live: those with upbeat autobiographies lived more than ten years longer than those whose language was more neutral.Deborah Danner, a psychologist at the University of Kentucky who spearheaded the study, noted that the results were particularly striking because all members of the convent lived similar lifestyles, eliminating many variables that normally make it difficult to interpret longevity studies.‘It was a phenomenal finding’, she says. ‘A researcher gets a finding like that maybe once in a lifetime.’ However, she points out that no one has been able to determine why positive emotions might have such life-extending effects.


B


Barbara Fredrickson, Professor of Psychology at the University of Michigan, believes that part of the answer is the ‘undo effect’. According to this theory, positive emotions help you live longer by shutting down the effects of negative ones. Fredrickson’s theory begins with the observation that negative emotions, like fear and stress, enhance our flight-or-fight response to very real threats. However, even when the emergency is gone, negative emotions produce lingering effects. Brooks Gump, a stress researcher at the State University of New York, explains that one of these effects is excessive cardiovascular reactivity. Behaviourally, Gump says, this reactivity is related to excessive vigilance: the state of being constantly on guard for potential dangers. Not only is it physically draining to live in a perpetual state of high vigilance, but high cardiovascular reactivity could be linked to increased chances of a heart attack.


C


Fredrickson believes positive emotions work their magic by producing a rapid unwinding of pent-up tension, restoring the system to normal. People who quickly bounce back from stress often speed the process by harnessing such emotions as amusement, interest, excitement, and happiness, she says. To test her theory, Fredrickson told a group of student volunteers that they had only a few minutes to prepare a speech that would be critiqued by experts. After letting the students get nervous about that, Fredrickson then told them they wouldn’t actually have to deliver their speeches. She monitored heart rates and blood pressure. Not surprisingly, all students got nervous about their speeches, but those who viewed the experiment with good-humored excitement saw their heart rates return to normal much more quickly than those who were angry about being fooled. In a second experiment, Fredrickson reported that even those who normally were slow to bounce back could be coached to recover more quickly by being told to view the experiment as a challenge, rather than a threat.


D


Fredrickson believes that positive emotions make people more flexible and creative. Negative emotions, she says, give a heightened sense of detail that makes us hypersensitive to minute clues related to the source of a threat. But that also produces ‘tunnel vision’ in which we ignore anything unrelated to the danger. Fredrickson speculated that just as positive emotions can undo the cardiovascular effects of negative ones, they may also reverse the attention-narrowing effects of negative feelings: broadening our perspectives.


E


To verify her theory, Fredrickson showed a group of students some film clips–some saw frightening clips, some saw humorous ones or peaceful ones. They then did a matching test in which they were shown a simple drawing and asked which of two other drawings it most resembled. The drawings were designed so that people would tend to give one answer if they focused on details, and another answer if they focused on the big picture. The results confirmed Fredrickson’s suspicion that positive emotions affect our perceptions. Students who had seen the humorous or peaceful clips were more likely to match objects according to broad impressions.


F


This fits with the role that positive emotions might have played in early human tribes, Fredrickson says. Negative emotions provided focus, which was important for surviving in life-or-death situations, but the ability to feel positive emotions was of long-term value because it opened the mind to new ideas. Humour is a good example of this. She says: ‘The emotions are transient, but the resources are durable. If you building a friendship through being playful, that friendship is a lasting resource.’ So while the good feelings may pass, the friendship remains. On an individual level, Fredrickson’s theory also says that taking time to do things that make you feel happy isn’t simply self-indulgent. Not only are these emotions good for the individual, but they are also good for society.


G


Other researchers are intrigued by Fredrickson’s findings. Susan Folkman, of the University of California, has spent two decades studying how people cope with long-term stresses such as bereavement, or caring for a chronically ill child. Contrary to what one might expect, she says, these people frequently experience positive emotions. ‘These emotions aren’t there by accident’, she adds. ‘Mother Nature doesn’t work that way, I think that they give a person time out from the intense stress to restore their resources and keep going. This is very consistent with Fredrickson’s work.’

groups.title: Questions 14-26
groups.source_html:
Questions 14 – 19


Look at the seven sections (A – G). Which section contains the following information?

You may choose any letter more than once.

Question / ParagraphABCDEFG
14

14 a conclusion that it is possible to train people to deal with anxiety
✅ 正确答案：C

定位关键信息：train, deal with anxiety


原文：C段, “In a second experiment, Fredrickson reported that even those who normally were slow to bounce back could be coached to recover more quickly by being told to view the experiment as a challenge, rather than a threat.” 


解析：原文提到了教授训练学生应对焦虑的案例，在第二个实验中，即使是情绪恢复缓慢的人也可以通过被告知将实验视为挑战而不是威胁来更快地恢复过来。
15

15 conclusive evidence that lifespan can be influenced by emotions
✅ 正确答案：A

定位关键信息：evidence, lifespan


原文：A段, “The researchers found that, although the young women were in their early twenties when they wrote their essays, the emotions expressed in these writings were predictive of how long they would live: those with upbeat autobiographies lived more than ten years longer than those whose language was more neutral.” 


解析：原文提到那些写下积极向上自传的女性比使用中性语言的女性多活了十年以上。
16

16 an explanation of the way negative emotions affect what people concentrate on
✅ 正确答案：D

定位关键信息：negative emotions, concentrate one


原文：D段, “Negative emotions, she says, give a heightened sense of detail that makes us hypersensitive to minute clues related to the source of a threat. But that also produces ‘tunnel vision’ in which we ignore anything unrelated to the danger.” 


解析：原文提到负面情绪会是我们对与威胁相关的细节极度敏感，同时忽视与威胁无关的信息。
17

17 an experiment that showed how a positive outlook can help people adjust to a stressful situation faster than others
✅ 正确答案：C

定位关键信息：positive outlook, adjust, faster


原文：C段, “Not surprisingly, all students got nervous about their speeches, but those who viewed the experiment with good-humored excitement saw their heart rates return to normal much more quickly than those who were angry about being fooled.” 


解析：原题提到那些以轻松愉快心态看待实验的学生其心率恢复正常的速度比那些因被捉弄而生气的学生要快得多。
18

18 a discovery beyond what researchers were investigating
✅ 正确答案：A

定位关键信息：discovery, beyond


原文：A段, “The researchers were seeking material to confirm earlier studies hinting at a link between having a good vocabulary in youth and a low risk of Alzheimer’s disease in old age. What they found was even more amazing. The researchers found that, although the young women were in their early twenties when they wrote their essays, the emotions expressed in these writings were predictive of how long they would live: those with upbeat autobiographies lived more than ten years longer than those whose language was more neutral.” 


解析：原文提到实验原本研究词汇量和老年痴呆的关系，结果发现了情绪乐观的人寿命更长。
19

19 an experiment where the nature of a material seen by participants affected the way they performed a task
✅ 正确答案：E

定位关键信息：nature of a material，performed a task


原文：E段, “To verify her theory, Fredrickson showed a group of students some film clips – some saw frightening clips, some saw humorous ones or peaceful ones. ”和“The results confirmed Fredrickson’s suspicion that positive emotions affect our perceptions. Students who had seen the humorous or peaceful clips were more likely to match objects according to broad impressions.” 


解析：原文提到Fredrickson让一些学生看惊悚的电影片段，一些学生看幽默或平和的片段。结果验证了Fredrickson的猜想，观看幽默或平和片段的学生更倾向于根据整体印象来匹配物品。


同义替换：nature of a material=frightening clips/humorous ones/peaceful ones, participants=students

Questions 20 – 23


Choose the correct researchers(A – D) for each statement. You may choose any correct researcher more than once.


List of Researchers

A Deborah Danner

B Barbara Fredrickson

C Brooks Gump

D Susan Folkman

Question / ParagraphABCD
20

20 People whose daily lives are stressful often have surprisingly positive emotions. 
✅ 正确答案：D

定位关键信息：daily lives, stressful


原文：G段, “Other researchers are intrigued by Fredrickson’s findings. Susan Folkman, of the University of California, has spent two decades studying how people cope with long-term stresses such as bereavement, or caring for a chronically ill child. Contrary to what one might expect, she says, these people frequently experience positive emotions” 


解析：原文提到Susan Folkman花了二十年时间研究人们如何应对长期压力，如失去亲人或照顾长期患病的孩子。研究结果出人意料，这些人常常会感受到积极的情绪。


同义替换：stressful=stress
21

21 The body’s reaction to a crisis may trigger a life-threatening event. 
✅ 正确答案：C

定位关键信息：Brooks Gump


原文：B段, “Behaviourally, Gump says, this reactivity is related to excessive vigilance: the state of being constantly on guard for potential dangers. Not only is it physically draining to live in a perpetual state of high vigilance, but high cardiovascular reactivity could be linked to increased chances of a heart attack.” 


解析：原文提到长期处于警惕危险的状态可能导致心脏病。


同义替换：crisis=danger, life-threatening event= a heart attack
22

22 It is unusual to have a study group whose circumstances were very alike. 
✅ 正确答案：A

定位关键信息：Deborah Danner


原文：A段, “Deborah Danner, a psychologist at the University of Kentucky who spearheaded the study, noted that the results were particularly striking because all members of the convent lived similar lifestyles, eliminating many variables that normally make it difficult to interpret longevity studies.” 


解析：原文提到Deborah Danner研究的修女们生活方式相似，消除了很多通常会影响长寿研究的变量。


同义替换：similar=alike, circumstance=lifestyles
23

23 The reasons for a link between positive emotions and a longer life have not been established. 
✅ 正确答案：A

定位关键信息：Deborah Danner

 

原文：A段, “However, she points out that no one has been able to determine why positive emotions might have such life-extending effects.”

 

解析：原文提到Deborah Danner指出还没有人能够确定为什么积极的情绪会增加寿命。

 

同义替换：

a longer life=life-extending

Questions 24 – 26


Complete the sentences. Write ONE WORD ONLY in each gap.

24

In early tribes, negative emotions gave humans the that they needed to deal with emergencies.

正确答案： 24 focus
Question 24

定位关键信息：early tribes, negative emotions, emergencies.


原文：F段, “This fits with the role that positive emotions might have played in early human tribes, Fredrickson says. Negative emotions provided focus, which was important for surviving in life-or-death situations,…” 


解析：原文提到，Fredrickson认为负面情绪能够让人专注，这在生死攸关的情境中非常重要。


同义替换：emergencies=life-or-death situations

25

Fredrickson believes that a passing positive emotion can lead to an enduring asset such as a , which is useful in times to come.

正确答案： 25 friendship
Question 25

定位关键信息：Fredrickson, positive emotion, enduring asset


原文：F段，“Humour is a good example of this. She says: ‘The emotions are transient, but the resources are durable. If you building a friendship through being playful, that friendship is a lasting resource.’”


解析：原文提到如果你通过玩乐建立了一段友谊，那么这段友谊就成了一种持久的资源。因此，虽然美好的感觉可能会消逝，但友谊却会一直存在。 


同义替换：

positive emotion=humour, enduring=lasting, asset=resource

26

Fredrickson also believes that both individuals and benefit from positive emotions.

正确答案： 26 society
Question 26

定位关键信息：Fredrickson, individuals


原文：F段, “On an individual level, Fredrickson’s theory also says that taking time to do things that make you feel happy isn’t simply self-indulgent. Not only are these emotions good for the individual, but they are also good for society.” 


解析：原文提到Fredrickson认为乐观的情绪有益于个人和社会。


同义替换：benefit=good
